notes.note: MPLS LDP enabled with 'mpls ldp router-id <loopback>' and 'mpls ip' under interfaces. Supports LDP-IGP synchronization with 'mpls ldp sync'.
MPLS LDP is a core feature. Configured under 'mpls ldp' with interface and address-family sub-modes. Supports LDP-IGP sync, session protection, and graceful restart natively.

IOS-XR platforms increasingly use Segment Routing (SR-MPLS) instead of LDP for label distribution. SR eliminates the need for LDP sessions. SR uses IGP (IS-IS or OSPF) as the only control plane — prefix-SIDs are encoded as indices from the SRGB base (default 16000-23999). The data plane is identical: 'show mpls forwarding' makes no distinction between LDP and SR labels.

TRAP: When migrating from LDP to SR, 'sr-prefer' must be enabled under the IGP to prefer SR labels over LDP labels. Without it, LDP labels remain in the forwarding table even when SR labels are available. Verify with 'show cef <prefix>' — check which label source is active.

TRAP: Enabling Ti-LFA on interfaces that previously used Remote-LFA (R-LFA) will tear down R-LFA targeted LDP sessions. If pseudowires or L2VPN services depend on those targeted sessions, they will be disrupted. Plan Ti-LFA migration carefully and explicitly configure needed targeted LDP sessions before enabling Ti-LFA.

SR does not support LAN links for SR-TE path computation. All interfaces participating in SR-TE must have 'point-to-point' configured under IS-IS/OSPF. Missing this on a switch-connected link (which appears as a LAN segment) is a common and subtle cause of 'no path found' in SR-TE policies.
MPLS requires 'feature mpls ldp'. LDP configured under 'mpls ldp configuration'. NX-OS 9.3+ supports MPLS on Nexus 9000 with specific linecards/ASICs. Not all Nexus models support MPLS.
